title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Fast Food on Local Food Cultures

texts.A: The global spread of fast-food chains is a form of 'culinary colonialism' that is erasing the flavor and diversity of our planet. When every city in the world has the same burger and fried chicken outlets, we lose the unique traditions that have been passed down for generations. This is not just about food; it is about the loss of the social structures—the family meal, the local market—that these traditions supported. The efficiency of the assembly line is no substitute for the soul of the kitchen.
texts.B: The 'threat' of fast food to local culture is largely a myth. In most countries, international chains have had to adapt their menus to local tastes, creating a hybrid cuisine that is actually quite innovative. Furthermore, these chains provide safe, affordable, and consistent food options for people on the move. Fast food is not replacing traditional cooking; it is simply providing a convenient alternative for a busy, urbanized population. Local food cultures are resilient and can coexist perfectly well with global brands.
texts.C: I agree that the 'homogenization' of global tastes is a valid concern, but the blame lies more with the industrialization of agriculture than with any specific restaurant chain. I share the view that fast food has damaged our social fabric, but this is a symptom of a faster-paced lifestyle, not the cause. We should focus on 'food education'—teaching people the value of local ingredients and the history of their own cuisine—so that they can make informed choices rather than simply banning certain types of business.
texts.D: The success of fast-food giants is a testament to the power of the market. People choose these options because they offer better value and consistency than many traditional alternatives. While I share the nostalgia for local markets, we must recognize that they were often inaccessible to the working classes. Fast food has 'democratized' eating out. The real issue is the health impact—the high levels of salt, sugar, and fat—not the 'cultural' impact. We should regulate the ingredients, not the brand's presence on our streets.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the resilience of local food traditions in the face of fast-food expansion?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes local cultures are 'resilient' and can 'coexist.' Expert A argues that fast food is 'erasing the flavor and diversity' and replacing unique traditions.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the loss of social structures associated with traditional dining?
questions.explanation: Expert A laments the loss of the 'family meal' and 'social structures.' Expert C agrees that 'fast food has damaged our social fabric.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the economic accessibility (democratization) of fast food?
questions.explanation: Expert D says fast food has 'democratized' eating out and offers 'better value.' Expert B also mentions it provides 'affordable' options for people on the move.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on what the 'real issue' with fast food is?
questions.explanation: Expert D argues the health impact is the real issue, not the cultural one. Expert A argues the cultural impact ('culinary colonialism') and the loss of diversity is the fundamental problem.
